passages.title: What is an unfair advantage in sport?
passages.source_html:
What is an unfair advantage in sport?


Olympic athletes increasingly depend on technology to help them win – but is that fair?


A


What happened to the Australian athlete Ron Clarke in the 10,000 metres at the Mexico City Olympics of 1968 is now virtually forgotten, though at the time it was headline news. Clarke was the greatest distance runner in history. He'd broken more world records than anybody else. But in front of 55,000 horrified spectators, the event went disastrously wrong. In the third lap, one runner keeled over and with six laps to go, two more were carried away. Yet the race was being conducted at a relatively leisurely speed: the halfway time was the slowest since the Paris Olympics of 1924. With two laps to go, Clarke was in the leading pack. 'I'd never felt better in a race,' he says. But suddenly he too began to struggle, and as the frontrunners moved up a gear, a gap opened up. Clarke remembers nothing of his last lap which he ran in 90 seconds 'Normally I would run it in 64,' he explains. He stumbled across the line in sixth place and collapsed. He was administered oxygen and stretchered off the track.


B


Mexico City is surrounded by mountains and is over 2,240 metres above sea level. That the altitude would have an impact on the Games was predicted. Clarke had raised the issue himself, but had been told by the Australian sports authorities that complaining was regarded as bad sportsmanship. As it turned out, he had good reason to do so. Clearly, the link between athletic performance and altitude needed further investigation.


C


Although there were few standout performances in distance running at the Mexico Games, they marked a turning point: the start of an astonishing record of success by east Africans. While Clarke lay crumpled in a heap, runners from Kenya and Ethiopia were celebrating their gold and silver medals. The record books confirm how entrenched this pattern has become. The names of the seven fastest men in history over 5,000 metres are Bekele, Gebrselassie, Komen, Kipchoge, Sihinetzx, Songkok and Chereno. They are all from either Kenya or Ethiopia. Between 1997 and 2011 the 10,000 metres men's world record was smashed five times, dropping from 26:31.32 to 26:17.53. Each time, the record was broken by a Kenyan or an Ethiopian. While there is a complex mix of economic, political, social and cultural explanations for the pre-eminence of east Africans, one factor is surely that many of these athletes have lived most of their lives in thin air.


D


At high altitudes, a number of physiological alterations occur. Most importantly, more red blood cells and haemoglobin are produced. This, in turn, increases the capacity of the blood to carry oxygen, which feeds the muscles and which gives an advantage to the athletes when they return to sea level. However, it is impossible to train with the same level of intensity in the mountains – aerobic capacity and cardio-respiratory function both suffer at altitude. As a result, the consensus is that the optimum approach to athletic preparation is: Live High, Train low (LHTL). Yet that has obvious practical drawbacks. Not many people live in the mountains and those who do would prefer not to spend several hours each day driving up and down winding treacherous roads.


E


That's where the altitude tent – sometimes called the hypoxic tent – comes in. Around two decades ago, two different scientists had the same exciting thought. If they could artificially control the atmosphere within a confined space, they could simulate the effects of high altitude and save an athlete at sea-level from the time and expense of travelling to higher ground. Altitude tents have improved over the years: they're not as hot or as noisy as the early prototypes, and are much cheaper too. They are also perfectly lawful. Five years ago, when the tents were investigated by WADA (the World Anti-Doping Agency), it was ruled that they did not violate the spirit of distance running. It is now routine for athletes to sleep in them in preparation for an event.


F


However, it is not the case that all new technologies gain approval. In 2008, a staggering 105 world records were broken in swimming. The vast majority were achieved by competitors wearing the new Speedo LZR Racer suit. These suits use a high-tech fabric tested in Nasa's wind tunnels, which reduces drag and improves buoyancy. The LZR was initially sanctioned by FINA, the international swimming body. But as better suits were produced by Speedo and other manufacturers, and more records were broken, they became increasingly controversial. In a 2009 ruling, FINA changed its mind banning all suits made with this high-tech fabric.


G


Going faster, higher, stronger is integral to the logic of athletics in general, and the Olympics in particular. Athletes believe they need records all the time. And the only way they can achieve records is by improving the clothing, the kit, the training, the nutrition – all to identify minute distinctions between people of 0.0001 of a second. But when a new technology is invented, the relevant sports authority has to consider whether to embrace or reject it. In some cases, athletes are granted permission to use the technology; in others, it is banned. But whatever the outcome, rulings should not appear arbitrary: arguments have to be examined and weighed and the rules of logic ought to apply in every case.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14 - 20


The text has seven paragraphs. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph and move it into the gap.

Options (Word Bank):
A. Early research into athletes' physiology
B. A convenient method of acclimatization
C. The need for a rational approach
D. Changes in the body
E. The athletes who break the rules
F. Well-founded concerns
G. The surprising outcome of a race
H. The reversal of a decision
I. The runners who dominate
14
正确答案： 14 G. The surprising outcome of a race  |  15 F. Well-founded concerns  |  16 I. The runners who dominate  |  17 D. Changes in the body  |  18 B. A convenient method of acclimatization  |  19 H. The reversal of a decision  |  20 C. The need for a rational approach
Question 14

对应原文： A段，What happened to the Australian athlete Ron Clarke in the 10,000 metres at the Mexico City Olympics of 1968 is now virtually forgotten, though at the time it was headline news.

But suddenly he too began to struggle, and as the frontrunners moved up a gear, a gap opened up. Clarke remembers nothing of his last lap which he ran in 90 seconds


详细解析： A段指出，Ron Clarke在1968年墨西哥城奥运会上意外失利的经历，原本他是被认为能够胜任的跑步者，但在比赛中却出现了意外情况，最后甚至不得不接受氧气治疗。这是一场“令人意外的比赛结果”。A段的主旨句是“What happened to the Australian athlete Ron Clarke in the 10,000 metres at the Mexico City Olympics of 1968”。

Question 15

对应原文：B段， "Mexico City is surrounded by mountains and is over 2,240 metres above sea level. That the altitude would have an impact on the Games was predicted. Clarke had raised the issue himself, but had been told by the Australian sports authorities that complaining was regarded as bad sportsmanship."


详细解析：B段指出了墨西哥城的高海拔可能会影响运动员表现的问题，Clarke自己也曾提出担忧，但他的担忧被忽视了。这说明他对于海拔的担忧是有理有据的 B段的主旨句是“That the altitude would have an impact on the Games was predicted Clarke had raised the issue himself, but had been told by the Australian sports authorities that complaining was regarded as bad sportsmanship. ”。


同义替换：well-founded concerns=the altitude would have an impact

Question 16

对应原文： C段，"Although there were few standout performances in distance running at the Mexico Games, they marked a turning point: the start of an astonishing record of success by east Africans."

They are all from either Kenya or Ethiopia.


详细解析：C段指出了东非运动员在长跑比赛中的卓越表现，以及他们在此项赛事中的主导地位C段的主旨句是“Although there were few standout performances in distance running at the Mexico Games, they marked a turning point: the start of an astonishing record of success by east Africans”。

Question 17

对应原文： D段，"At high altitudes, a number of physiological alterations occur. Most importantly, more red blood cells and haemoglobin are produced. This, in turn, increases the capacity of the blood to carry oxygen, which feeds the muscles and which gives an advantage to the athletes when they return to sea level. "


详细解析：D段指出，高海拔环境对人体生理的改变，尤其是红血球和血红蛋白的增加，从而增加血液的携氧能力。这些都是身体的生理变化。D段的主旨句是“At high altitudes, a number of physiological alterations occur, most importantly.”。


同义替换：changes=alterations; in the body=physiological

Question 18

对应原文： E段，"That's where the altitude tent – sometimes called the hypoxic tent – comes in. Around two decades ago, two different scientists had the same exciting thought. If they could artificially control the atmosphere within a confined space, they could simulate the effects of high altitude and save an athlete at sea-level from the time and expense of travelling to higher ground. "


详细解析： E段指出了海拔帐篷的发明及其作用，即帮助运动员在低海拔环境中模拟高海拔效果，使他们能够更便捷地适应高海拔环境。E段的主旨句是“That's where the altitude tent-sometimes called the hypoxic tent-comes in Around two decades ago, two different scientists had the same exciting thought.”。

Question 19

对应原文：F段， "In 2008, a staggering 105 world records were broken in swimming.

But as better suits were produced by Speedo and other manufacturers, and more records were broken, they became increasingly controversial. In a 2009 ruling, Fina changed its mind banning all suits made with this high-tech fabric."


详细解析：F段指出了新款游泳衣在破纪录方面引起的争议，以及国际泳联最初批准后又改变决定禁止这些游泳衣的过程。F段的主旨句是“In a 2009 ruling, Fina changed its mind banning all suits made with this high-tech fabric.”。


同义替换：reversal=changed its mind; decision=ruling

Question 20

对应原文： G段，"But when a new technology is invented, the relevant sports authority has to consider whether to embrace or reject it. In some cases, athletes are granted permission to use the technology; in others, it is banned. But whatever the outcome, rulings should not appear arbitrary: arguments have to be examined and weighed and the rules of logic ought to apply in every case."


详细解析： G段指出了当新技术出现时，体育管理机构需要合理评估是否允许使用该技术，以确保决定的合理性和一致性。G段的主旨句是“But whatever the outcome, rulings should not appear arbitrary: arguments have to be examined and weighed and the rules of logic ought to apply in every case.”。


同义替换：rational approach=examined and weighed; need=consider

Questions 21 - 22


Choose TWO correct answers.

Options (Word Bank):
A. Clarke was not performing well immediately prior to the Mexico Games.
B. The worries Clarke had before the Mexico Games were not taken into account.
C. Clarke's experiences at the Mexico Games are widely talked about today.
D. At one stage of the Mexico Games 10,000 metres Clarke was near the front.
E. Clarke was the only runner at the Mexico Games who appeared to be affected by the altitude.
21

Which TWO of the following statements about Ron Clarke are made in the passage?

正确答案： 1 B. The worries Clarke had before the Mexico Games were not taken into account.  |  2 D. At one stage of the Mexico Games 10,000 metres Clarke was near the front.

定位信息： worries, not taken into account, near the front, Mexico Games 10,000 metres


对应原文：对于选项 B，B段，"Clarke had raised the issue himself, but had been told by the Australian sports authorities that complaining was regarded as bad sportsmanship."对于选项 D，A段，"With two laps to go, Clarke was in the leading pack."


解题思路：
B段指出，克拉克对高海拔的担忧（raised the issue），但澳大利亚体育当局并没有重视他的担忧，认为抱怨是不符合体育精神的表现。这说明他的担忧未被重视。

A段指出比赛还剩两圈时克拉克在领先队伍中（in the leading pack），说明他在比赛的某个阶段处于前列。


同义替换：worries=raised the issue；near the front=in the leading pack； at one stage=with two laps to go

Questions 23 - 26


Complete the summary. Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

23

Do all new technologies gain approval?

Some people may be puzzled by attitudes towards performance-enhancing technologies in sport. For example, why is the altitude tent considered acceptable, but not the LZR Racer suit? For distance running, WADA concluded that the altitude tent was not contrary to the  of the sport. 


However, the LZR swimsuit, which is made from a special fabric that aids buoyancy and cuts down , was banned. Athletes think they have to continually set fresh  . This is made possible by better  and training, as well as improved clothing and equipment. However, when sports authorities have to decide whether to give permission for a new performance-enhancing technology to be used, it is important that their decisions are not seen as arbitrary.

正确答案： 23 spirit  |  24 drag  |  25 records  |  26 nutrition
Question 23

定位信息： WADA, altitude tent,not contrary to


对应原文： E段，"Five years ago, when the tents were investigated by WADA (the World Anti-Doping Agency), it was ruled that they did not violate the spirit of distance running. "


解题思路：  E段指出，WADA 的调查认为高原帐篷“did not violate the spirit of distance running”（不违反跑步精神）。


同义替换：not contrary to=did not violate

Question 24

定位信息： LZR swimsuit, cuts down


对应原文： F段，"These suits use a high-tech fabric tested in Nasa's wind tunnels, which reduces drag and improves buoyancy."


解题思路： F段指出，这种高科技面料能够“reduces drag”（减少阻力）和“improves buoyancy”（增加浮力）。


同义替换：cuts down=reduces

Question 25

定位信息： continually set fresh


对应原文：G段， "Athletes believe they need records all the time, and the only way is through minute changes of 0.0001 of a second. "


详细解析： G段指出运动员认为他们“需要不断创造记录”（need records all the time）。


同义替换：continually set fresh=need records all the time

Question 26

定位信息： better,training


对应原文： G段， And the only way they can achieve records is by improving the clothing, the kit, the training, the nutrition – all to identify minute distinctions between people of 0.0001 of a second. 


解题思路： G段指出创造纪录的唯一途径，就是不断改进服装、器材、训练和营养。
